All right, you're back. Here's what we're going to do now. I want to throw up a graphic of the straight line. Let's put it up right now. And I want you to notice exactly where you are on the straight line, which is at this point, this dot right here. Okay, check that out. This is flashing dot right there. See what happened is you hit with an objection and you then deflected that objection. By saying, I hear what you're saying, but let me ask you a question. Does the idea make sense to you? Do you like the idea and that money aside, hypothetical tonality would tend to create a more honest, positive response from your prospect versus you saying, well, does the idea make sense to you? They'll say, whoa, no, right? Which is very important because you want to make sure you get an accurate read of where they are on the circular scale. They see simply put, while a basic yes is enough to move forward during the front half of the sale, you need this enthusiastic guess to move forward in the back half of the sale. And again, the reason for this is that his level of her level of enthusiasm is going to serve as your primary means of measuring where they land on the circular scale for each of the three 10s. For example, suppose that in response to deflecting Bill's initial objection, he replied in an ambivalent tone of, that sounds pretty good. Let me ask you this. Where does that response set in that ambivalent tone place Bill on the certain scale? Is he at a three, a five, a nine, a 10? Now I'm not going to play the whole interactivity game. I tried it, but it was just a bit too complicated. So let me just kind of narrate the whole thing. You see, if your prospect is at a 10, which he's not in this case, you will definitely know it. His response will sound something like, oh yeah, I mean, absolutely this makes total sense to me. I love the idea. In other words, his positive bias will be so strong that his words and his tone will make this ultra high level of certainty a dead giveaway. And by the way, the same is true of a one on the certain scale, albeit in the exact opposite direction. See, in this case, his response will sound something like, oh no, not at all. I think this is the stupidest idea I have ever heard. And his tone obviously be one of utter disgust or contempt, right? And while those levels in between are a bit more challenging to hit directly on the nose, he's clearly not at a two or three, as either one of those would have far more negative emotion attached to it, right? His response. And he's clearly not at a nine or an eight, as either one of those levels would have far more positive emotion attached to the response. So then where is he? Where is Bill on the certain scale right now, based on that ambivalent response? Well, I wasn't kidding when I said it's a bit hard to hit those middle numbers directly on the nose. But still, based on what he said is words and his accompanying tonality, I would say somewhere around a five or a six, probably no lower than that. Although I'm not really sure exactly, but due to his ambivalence, which struck me as more positive than negative, I would probably say that based on all my years of experience and estimating my prospects varying levels of certainty, I probably put Bill at a six as opposed to a five, but either one will be fine. Now if you're wondering why I just gave you a long winded explanation like that, I'm driving home a very important point. Namely, that looping is as much of an art as it is a science. So don't make yourself crazy here trying to figure out their exact level of certainty. It's not necessary. The approximate level is good enough and you're about to see that right now. So watch, the fact that I pegged Bill at about a six on the certainty scale, let me ask you a question. Does it make sense for me to move forward on the straight line now towards the close? Answer yes or no. Right now, should I move forward to the close with the six on the certainty scale? So you think I should move forward? The answer is absolutely not and I'll tell you why. Bingo, you're absolutely right. The answer is no, you definitely would not move forward and let me tell you exactly why. A six is not nearly high enough on the certainty scale for Bill or for that matter any prospect to seriously consider parting with their hard earned money to buy something. And that's sure if they're buying $300,000 of Microsoft stock or $500 of a penny stock. A $120,000 Gordres Mercedes-Benz S-Class or a $500 10-speed bicycle. A $90,000 state of the art home theater system or $300 flat screen TV. Get the point? Doesn't really matter. So in light of that, with that lack of certainty present, instead of moving forward and trying to close the deal, what we do instead is we loop back now to the front half of the sale to the exact point on the straight line where we left off when we finished the main body of our sales presentation. And now we're going to make a follow up presentation that exactly builds on the airtight logical case that we framed during your initial presentation. You get those we framed and didn't front load. So we had this frame in place, right? Well, guess what? Our follow up presentation is now going to add on to that frame and make it tire in essence. That follow up presentation picks up right where your logical frame left off and it uses your most powerful benefits, your most coherent assertions to turn that frame into an irrefutable, inarguable scream from the hilltop airtight logical case while use the advanced tonality technique of pace, pace, lead to start building emotional certainty as well. See with this one particular pattern, what you want to accomplish here is two things simultaneously. First, you're looking to increase your prospects level of logical certainty to as close to a 10 as possible for that first 10. And second, you're looking to begin the process of also increasing your prospects emotional search of that first 10 as much as possible. So let's go through this process step by step, starting with Bill's response, which landed at a six on the logical certainty scale due to his ambivalent tone. Specifically, Bill had said, yeah, it sounds pretty good. So to that, your standard straight line response would be exactly. It really is a great situation. In fact, one of the true beauties here is, and then you'll go directly from there into the main body of your follow up presentation. Now slow this down for a second here. I responded by saying exactly. It really is a phenomenal idea. In fact, the true beauty is, right? Now watch what happens if Bill would have responded at a four or 4.5. I would have said exactly. It really is a great idea. In fact, one of the true beauties here is, and if Bill would have responded at a seven, I would have said exactly. It really is a great idea. In fact, one of the true beauties is, ah, you see the pattern here? You see in the same way as deflection, we always responded with the same words. We're doing the exact same thing again. You see every sale is the same. It's pretty powerful stuff. In other words, what's happening here is I'm always going to respond to Bill's words in exactly the same way. But what's going to change is the tonality that I apply to those words. For example, if Bill's response lands him at a six on the certainty scale, that'll be like, yeah, it sounds pretty good. What I wouldn't say is exactly, Bill. In reality, he'd say, whoa, good Lord, this guy doesn't hear me. He can't do that. Conversely, if Bill had a, oh my God, yeah, it sounds pretty good. I'll actually then down-regulate his, he'll say, what's the guy that didn't hear me? In other words, see when Bill responds to you in that ambivalent tone, he knows full well what his tone is saying. Tonality is powerful. He's letting you know where he lands on that certainty scale. And the key here, now remember from, I believe it was the tonality module, either that or unconscious communication, either one, I'll check it out later, I'll find out. I had that story about Carter when he was playing soccer and came home really, really pissed off about that kid that was pushing around in the field. And I showed you why I use the tonality trick of pace, pace lead to essentially enter his world where he was. So he was really, really upset. So rather than saying, oh, Carter, what's wrong? He'd be like, get more upset. Instead of like, what's wrong, Carter? And I got even more angry than him. I entered his world where he was. And then I paced him. I paced him, meaning I remained angry. I spoke angry. And then I slowly calmed down. And I said, what do you think we should do here, Carter? He's like, I don't know that. He followed my words. That's the power of pacing and leading. Now let's apply it to this situation and create massive emotional certainty by doing something really cool. We're going to apply that to our language pattern. Here's what I mean. Because Bill replied in a way that put him at a six on the certain scale, more or less, right? I'm going to enter his world at just above a six, like a 6.2, 6.3, nudging him ever so slightly in the direction I want him to go. But I'm still entering his world pretty much exactly where he is. And from there, as I transition into the main body of my follow up presentation, I'm going to pace him and pace him and then lead him in the direction tonally, which means emotionally that I want him to go by slowly but surely increasing the level of certainty in my tone, timing it. So I hit pretty much peak tonality about halfway through my pattern. And then I'll maintain that peak tonality straight through to the end. I mean peak tonality, absolute certainty straight through to the end. So what I want to do now is I'm going to actually take you through an example of how you pace and lead like this. I'm going to make up like bogus funny words, like actually ABC, XYZ, DEF. So here's how it looks, right? So Bill responds at this level six or so on the certain scale. It says, yeah, it sounds pretty good, right? Six on the certain scale. But he wants to think about it. I say, exactly. It really is a great idea. That's a six. But let me say this, the true beauty is it also has, now I'm going to start building my aerotidal case in a mass way, it also has ABC. And on top of ABC, it also has DEF. And on top of DEF also has ABG, making up funnily. But what's my tonality like? I'm still at around a six. But watch what I do now. Let's say like 40, 30% of the way I hang. But then it also has XYZ one and ABC three and DEF four. And when you take all those things and look what's happening now, my words are becoming more certain. You put all of those things together and you combine the whole lot of them. And when you're done, it is just like the most unbelievable thing in the world. Bill, you see what I'm saying? Yeah, does that make sense to you? And that's how I close that pad now. He's like, oh yeah, exactly. Now what he'll do. And again, by the way, I can even begin to overestimate the importance of the actual word here, the language pattern. It needs to make perfect sense from every angle, mathematically, economically, logistically, the value proposition, your benefit stack, the pain resolution you offer, the energy expenditure, meaning energy in benefits out, your maximizer, your minimizer, your justifies, your powers, your comparisons, metaphors, your trustworthy figures. I want you to throw everything at this. And it must be flawlessly delivered using that strategy I showed you of pace, pace lead to create emotional certainty as well. And again, at the very end of that pattern, I completed it by asking my prospect the same leading question every time saying, you see what I'm saying? You build the idea. Like the idea. And remember, as I say those words, I'm maintaining that same peak tonality that I just got to from pace, pace leads right through to the end. You get it? Now, because of that, I've created emotional certainty through my tonality and this super airtight logical case I've created has created logical certainty. So I've accomplished something really, really cool there. I moved Bill both logically to a very, very high level. And emotionally, I began that process, probably give it to a seven or so, but I moved him emotionally as well. And at this point, I want to stop and have you take a quiz.
